title: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
summary: On 4 September 476 CE the Germanic warlord Odoacer entered Ravenna and deposed the teenage emperor Romulus Augustulus, whose father Orestes had crowned him only ten months earlier. Odoacer sent the imperial insignia east to Constantinople, took the title king of Italy, and pensioned the boy off to a fortress near Naples. Later ages fixed on that dry palace transaction as the end of the Roman Empire in the West, though the empire had been unravelling for a century and Roman institutions would outlast the last Caesar by generations.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
By the time Romulus Augustulus was crowned in the autumn of 475 the Roman Empire in the West had already been shrinking for three generations. The disaster at Adrianople in 378, where the Gothic cavalry of Fritigern destroyed a Roman field army and killed the emperor Valens, had opened the Balkans to Germanic settlement on terms that were never fully reversed. In 410 Alaric's Visigoths sacked Rome itself, a moral shock so great that Augustine of Hippo composed the City of God in response. The Vandals under Geiseric crossed the Strait of Gibraltar in 429, carved a kingdom out of Roman North Africa, and in 439 seized Carthage, cutting off the grain and tax revenues on which the western treasury depended. In 455 Geiseric's fleet sacked Rome a second time and carried the empress Licinia Eudoxia to Carthage as a hostage. Britain had been abandoned by the regular army around 410; much of Gaul was controlled by Visigothic and Burgundian federate kings; the Rhine frontier had effectively dissolved after the great crossing of the winter of 406.

Inside this collapsing perimeter the western court had become a puppet stage managed by successive Germanic generalissimos. The most durable was Ricimer, a half-Suevic, half-Gothic soldier who from 456 to 472 made and unmade emperors from his headquarters at Ravenna, the marsh-protected capital to which Honorius had transferred the court in 402. Ricimer elevated Majorian, then killed him; installed Libius Severus; recognised the eastern nominee Anthemius, then besieged and executed him in 472; and died a few weeks later, leaving the western throne to another cycle of coups. In 474 the eastern emperor Leo I sent his kinsman by marriage Julius Nepos to Ravenna as legitimate augustus of the West, and for a year Nepos governed a rump empire that in practice covered only Italy and a strip of Dalmatia across the Adriatic.

His undoing was his own general. Orestes, a Pannonian Roman who had earlier served as secretary to Attila the Hun, held the office of magister militum and commanded the field army in Italy, which was by now largely composed of Germanic soldiers, Heruli, Sciri, Turcilingi, and Rugians settled on Italian land as foederati. In August 475 Orestes marched on Ravenna, drove Nepos across the Adriatic to Salona in Dalmatia, and on 31 October raised his own son, a boy perhaps ten years old whose formal name was Romulus, to the imperial purple. The Senate obediently confirmed him. Contemporaries mockingly called him Augustulus, little Augustus, a diminutive that stuck and captured the disproportion between the great Julio-Claudian name and the shrunken office. The eastern court under Zeno never recognised him, continuing to regard Nepos in Dalmatia as the lawful western emperor.

The crisis came within a year. The federate soldiers of Italy demanded a third of the Italian countryside for their own, the same kind of land settlement Visigoths had been granted in Aquitaine two generations earlier. Orestes, an old Roman landowner, refused. The troops then rallied around one of their own officers, a Skiran or Thuringian named Odoacer, who was probably in his early forties and had served in the imperial guard. He promised his men the land they wanted. In late August 476 Odoacer's army marched on Ticinum, modern Pavia, where Orestes had taken refuge. The city was stormed, Orestes was captured and executed on 28 August, and his brother Paulus was killed a few days later outside Ravenna. Only the boy emperor remained, waiting in the palace by the pine forests north of the city.

image.caption: Gold tremissis of Romulus Augustus, minted at Rome between October 475 and September 476.
image.credit: Classical Numismatic Group, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On 4 September 476, according to the fifth-century Consularia Italica and later chroniclers, Odoacer entered Ravenna and formally deposed Romulus Augustulus. There was no battle in the city, no massacre, and, in a departure from Roman precedent, no execution of the deposed emperor. The boy was young enough that Odoacer, the historian Anonymus Valesianus tells us, took pity on him, spared his life, and sent him with his relatives to live at the Castellum Lucullanum, an old fortified villa on the Campanian coast near Naples that Lucullus had built four and a half centuries earlier. He was granted an annual pension of six thousand solidi, roughly the income of a wealthy senator. Nothing certain is known of him after that; a letter of Theodoric the Great from around 507 to a man called Romulus may or may not refer to the same person now grown middle-aged.

The more consequential act was Odoacer's disposal of the imperial insignia. According to the sixth-century historian Malchus of Philadelphia, whose account is preserved in later Byzantine excerpts, the Roman Senate at Odoacer's prompting drafted a letter to the eastern emperor Zeno in Constantinople. It announced that the West no longer required an emperor of its own; a single sovereign, Zeno, would suffice for the whole empire; and it asked that Odoacer be granted the rank of patricius to administer Italy in Zeno's name. The diadem, purple cloak, and other regalia of the western imperial office were physically shipped east across the Adriatic to Constantinople, where they were preserved as relics of a defunct court. Zeno was in an awkward position, since Julius Nepos still claimed to be the legitimate western augustus from Salona; he told the senators that they should recall Nepos, but sent Odoacer the patrician title anyway.

Odoacer at once assumed the style rex, king, on his coinage and in his charters, the first ruler in Italy for five centuries to hold that overtly non-Roman title. He kept the Roman civil administration, the Senate, the consulship, and the prefecture largely intact and continued to date documents by the consular year and to strike coins in the name of the eastern emperor. His Herulian, Skirian, and Rugian soldiers received the land allocation their revolt had been about, apparently in the form of a third of tax revenues rather than outright dispossession of Roman proprietors, though the arrangements varied region by region and are still argued over by scholars. He ruled Italy from Ravenna for seventeen years, campaigned in Dalmatia after Nepos was murdered by his own retainers in 480, and generally maintained a Romanised style of government.

His end came from another Germanic king. In 488 Zeno commissioned Theodoric, king of the Ostrogoths, to invade Italy and remove Odoacer, a convenient way of ridding the eastern Balkans of a dangerous army. Theodoric crossed the Julian Alps in 489, defeated Odoacer at the Isonzo and again at Verona, drove him back into Ravenna, and after a three-year siege of that lagoon fortress negotiated a joint kingship. On 15 March 493, at a banquet supposedly held to seal the reconciliation, Theodoric killed Odoacer with his own hand. The Ostrogothic kingdom of Italy, more elaborate and self-consciously Roman than Odoacer's, took his place. The last emperor's deposition seventeen years earlier was, by then, already ancient history.

image.caption: Romulus Augustulus resigns the imperial crown to Odoacer, engraving from Ridpath's Cyclopedia of Universal History.
image.credit: From John Clark Ridpath, Cyclopedia of Universal History, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
In Italy itself the year 476 changed less than later legend suggested. The Senate continued to meet, consuls were still elected annually and their names sent to Constantinople, taxes were collected in the old provinces, aqueducts and grain doles still supplied the city of Rome, and Latin remained the language of law and administration. Odoacer respected Catholic clergy though he was probably an Arian, and his kingdom minted coins in the names of first Zeno and then Anastasius. Under Theodoric after 493 this Romanised style of barbarian rule reached its apogee: Roman senators like Cassiodorus and Boethius served as high officials, monumental building resumed at Ravenna and Rome, and the Ostrogothic court patronised classical learning. To an educated inhabitant of fifth-century Rome the change from a distant Italian emperor to a distant Italian king may have felt more like an administrative rearrangement than the end of civilisation.

Beyond Italy the picture was harsher. The Vandal kingdom in North Africa persecuted Nicene Christians and taxed the old Roman landholders; the Visigoths in Aquitaine and Spain confiscated Roman estates; in Britain literacy, coinage, and urban life had already collapsed. Bryan Ward-Perkins in his 2005 book The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization has argued, on the basis of archaeological evidence from pottery, roof-tiles, and cattle bone sizes, that the material standard of living across much of the former western empire fell sharply between the fifth and seventh centuries, in some regions to Iron Age levels. Peter Heather in The Fall of the Roman Empire, also 2005, agrees that the collapse was a real catastrophe caused primarily by outside pressure from barbarian invaders, themselves often driven west by the Huns.

An older school of thought, associated most strongly with the Canadian medievalist Walter Goffart, has treated the fifth-century transition instead as a controlled accommodation between the Roman state and armed barbarian groups negotiated through legal frameworks that historians call the techniques of accommodation. On this reading, kingdoms like Odoacer's and Theodoric's were less a rupture than a transformation, and Roman institutions endured within them for generations. Modern scholarship has, broadly speaking, drawn from both traditions: the political apparatus of the West did fall in a real and irreversible way over the course of the fifth century, but daily life, religion, language, and law survived and mutated into the early medieval world.

The eastern half of the Roman Empire, meanwhile, lost nothing on 4 September 476 and would go on for almost a thousand more years. Constantinople, Zeno's capital, still ruled the Balkans, Anatolia, Syria, Egypt, and a strip of North Africa; its emperors called themselves Romans; its coins bore Roman legends; and in the sixth century Justinian would attempt, briefly and expensively, to reconquer the lost west. The eastern empire that later historians would call Byzantine is Roman continuity by another name.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The date 476 acquired its symbolic weight retrospectively. The sixth-century Italian bureaucrat Marcellinus Comes, writing in Constantinople in the reign of Justinian, was the first author to state plainly that the western Roman empire had perished with Romulus Augustulus, an observation he needed in order to justify Justinian's reconquest wars. Later chroniclers picked up the notice; Edward Gibbon in the eighteenth century gave it monumental form in The Decline and Fall of the Roman Empire, whose thirty-sixth chapter treats Odoacer's takeover as the pivot of world history. From Gibbon the date passed into schoolroom textbooks, and 476 is still the number most commonly given for the end of the ancient world and the beginning of the medieval.

Contemporaries would have found this puzzling. No one in Ravenna or Constantinople in 476 believed that Rome had ended. Julius Nepos was still alive; Zeno still ruled a Roman empire; Latin still flowed from every chancery. The Frankish, Ostrogothic, and Visigothic kings who succeeded in the West all claimed some form of Roman legitimacy, whether by taking the title of patricius, minting coins with imperial portraits, or, in the case of Charlemagne on Christmas Day 800, being crowned emperor of the Romans in the old basilica of St Peter. The idea of Rome as the universal political order haunted Europe for centuries; the Holy Roman Empire that dissolved only in 1806 was one long echo of this refusal to accept that the empire had ended.

Modern historians have therefore learned to speak more carefully. Rather than a fall in 476 they describe a fifth-century transformation stretching from the crossing of the Rhine in 406 to the Lombard invasion of Italy in 568, in which the political structures of the western empire disintegrated into a mosaic of successor kingdoms while economic and cultural life underwent a slower but genuine contraction in most regions. The debate between the catastrophist and the transformationist readings of these centuries is one of the most productive quarrels in the study of late antiquity, and it is a debate that Romulus Augustulus, whom no ancient source records as ever having spoken a word, quietly presides over from the shore of the bay of Naples.

worldContext.text: China in 476 was split between two rival dynasties. In the north the Tuoba Wei, or Northern Wei, ruled from Pingcheng under the young emperor Xiaowen, who would in 493 move his capital south to Luoyang and impose a sweeping programme of sinicisation on his originally steppe-nomadic aristocracy. In the south the Liu Song dynasty held the Yangtze valley from Jiankang, modern Nanjing, though its late emperors were increasingly weak; it would be overthrown in 479 by the general Xiao Daocheng, founder of the Southern Qi. Buddhism was spreading rapidly in both realms; the great cave temples at Yungang had begun in 460 and Longmen would follow in 493.

Northern India was passing out of the golden age of the Gupta Empire. Skandagupta, who had beaten off an early Hunnic invasion in the 450s, died around 467, and his successors ruled a diminishing realm as the Alchon Huns under kings like Khingila and later Toramana pressed down from the northwest. In the same generation, according to traditional dates, the mathematician-astronomer Aryabhata was born around 476, and would within a quarter century write the Aryabhatiya, containing an accurate value of pi, a theory of eclipses caused by the earth's shadow, and one of the earliest known statements of the earth's rotation on its axis. To the south the Vakataka dynasty patronised the great painted cave monasteries at Ajanta.

The Sasanian Persian empire, Rome's traditional rival, was in serious difficulty. King Peroz I was locked in a losing war on his eastern frontier with the Hephthalites, another wave of steppe Huns; in 484 he would fall in a disastrous defeat in Khurasan, killed with most of his army in a trench-trap, and Persia would pay a humiliating annual tribute for a generation. Vandal North Africa, ruled from Carthage by the aged and ferocious Geiseric, was the wealthiest of the western barbarian successor states and controlled the sea lanes of the western Mediterranean, its fleet raiding Sicily and southern Italy at will. Coptic Christianity was flourishing in Egypt in monasteries founded by disciples of Pachomius.

In the Ethiopian highlands the kingdom of Aksum was near the height of its power, its port of Adulis on the Red Sea a hub of trade linking the Mediterranean to India and its coinage among the earliest in the world to bear a Christian cross. Kaleb of Aksum, who would in the sixth century intervene militarily in Yemen against Jewish Himyarite persecutors of Christians, belonged to a dynasty already old in 476. Along the Sahel the earliest phases of the Ghana empire and of towns like Jenne-jeno were quietly emerging as centres of gold and salt exchange. In central Africa Bantu-speaking farmers continued a centuries-long migration south and east across the continent.

In Mesoamerica the great highland city of Teotihuacan in the Valley of Mexico, with a population perhaps as large as 125,000, was in the last century of its dominance; its Feathered Serpent Pyramid had been built centuries earlier and its trading networks reached to the Maya lowlands. In the Peten forests of what is now Guatemala the Classic Maya were entering an especially dynamic phase: at Tikal the king Chak Tok Ich'aak II had died in 508 was still ahead, while the rival dynasty at Calakmul was consolidating power. Copan and Palenque were about to begin their most productive royal reigns. Along the northern Peruvian coast the Moche state was building the great adobe pyramids at Cerro Blanco.

Austronesian-speaking sailors, whose ancestors had left Taiwan and the Philippines thousands of years earlier, had already reached most of western Polynesia; Samoa, Tonga, and Fiji had been settled for a millennium. The great eastern voyaging that would carry the descendants of these communities to the Marquesas, Hawaii, Rapa Nui, and eventually New Zealand was still to come, but the double-hulled canoes and stellar navigation techniques on which those voyages depended were being refined in the harbours of the western islands. In highland New Guinea intensive taro and yam gardening supported dense communities in the Wahgi valley whose beginnings archaeologists trace back thousands of years.

figures.name: Romulus Augustulus
figures.role: Last western Roman emperor, a boy of perhaps ten placed on the throne by his father in October 475 and deposed by Odoacer eleven months later
figures.credit: Classical Numismatic Group, via Wikimedia Commons (CC BY-SA 3.0)

figures.name: Odoacer
figures.role: Germanic officer of Sciran or Skirian origin who overthrew Romulus Augustulus, took the title king of Italy, and ruled Italy from Ravenna 476 to 493

figures.name: Orestes
figures.role: Pannonian Roman general and former secretary to Attila the Hun, father of Romulus Augustulus, executed at Pavia on 28 August 476

figures.name: Julius Nepos
figures.role: The last western emperor recognised as legitimate by the eastern court, ruled from Dalmatia after 475 until murdered in 480

figures.name: Zeno
figures.role: Eastern Roman emperor at Constantinople who received the western imperial insignia from Odoacer's ambassadors and later authorised Theodoric to invade Italy

images.caption: Romulus Augustulus resigns the imperial crown to Odoacer, engraving from Ridpath's Cyclopedia of Universal History, a nineteenth-century imagining of the events of September 476.
images.credit: From John Clark Ridpath, Cyclopedia of Universal History, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Gold tremissis of Romulus Augustus, minted at Rome between October 475 and September 476; the inscription reads D N ROMVL AVGVSTVS P F AG.
images.credit: Classical Numismatic Group, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Romulus Augustus resigns the crown before Odoacer, illustration from Charlotte M. Yonge's Young Folks' History of Rome (1880).
images.credit: Illustration from Charlotte M. Yonge, Young Folks' History of Rome (1880), via Wikimedia Commons

sources.title: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
The Fall of Rome and the End of Civilization
